1: “The whole world had one language and a common speech.” The unity of language before Babel is the state that Pentecost partially restores. The Babel narrative (vv1–9) is the anti-Pentecost: human unity deployed for self-glorification and divine-defiance, resulting in judgment and scattering. Pentecost is the Christological answer: the Spirit of Christ reunites the divided languages not by imposing one tongue but by speaking in all tongues simultaneously (Acts 2:6–11). The original unified language is not restored; something better is given — a unity that transcends language through the Spirit of the risen Christ.
2: “As people moved eastward.” The eastward movement — away from Eden, away from God — is the direction of post-fall humanity throughout Genesis (Genesis 3:24; Genesis 4:16). The people of Shinar move east into the plain and settle there, beginning the project of self-sufficient civilization. Christ’s mission is the reversal: he moves “westward” in the theological geography of Scripture — from heaven downward to earth, from divine presence into human exile — in order to bring humanity back from its eastern exile. The incarnation is God coming toward the eastward-moving rebels.
3: “Come, let us make bricks.” The human “let us” of Babel is the counterfeit of the divine “let us” of creation (Genesis 1:26) and of judgment (Genesis 11:7). Humanity mimics divine language and divine council to pursue a self-directed project. The contrast is between the divine “let us make man in our image” (Genesis 1:26) — God as creator giving life to others — and the human “let us make bricks” — humanity as self-creator building its own security. Christ as the Word through whom creation happened (John 1:3) is the divine “let us” who invites all people not to build their own towers but to enter his kingdom (Matthew 11:28).
4: “Let us build ourselves a city… so that we may make a name for ourselves.” The Babel project — self-aggrandizing urbanism as a defense against divine dispersion — is directly contrasted with God’s response to Abraham in the very next chapter (Genesis 12:2 — “I will make your name great”). The name humanity tries to make for itself by force, God gives freely through covenant. The ultimate “name above every name” (Philippians 2:9) is given to Christ — not through human achievement but through self-emptying obedience (Philippians 2:6–8). Babel’s name-making is the anti-gospel; Christ’s exaltation is its answer.
5: “But the LORD came down to see the city and the tower the people were building.” The ironic divine descent — the tower meant to reach heaven requires God to “come down” even to see it. The irony deepens in light of the incarnation: in Christ, God truly comes down — not to judge a human tower but to enter human life at its lowest point (Philippians 2:7–8). The “coming down” at Babel is judgment; the “coming down” at Bethlehem is grace. Both are divine descents; only one saves.
6: “Nothing they plan to do will be impossible for them.” God’s assessment of unified human capacity — not flattery but a recognition that unchecked human self-assertion will reach devastating ends. The same assessment, reversed, applies to those united in Christ: “I can do all this through him who gives me strength” (Philippians 4:13); “with God all things are possible” (Matthew 19:26). The capacity that Babel deployed for self-glorification is the capacity that Christ redeems and redirects toward the glory of God and the good of all.
7: “Come, let us go down and confuse their language.” The divine “let us” of judgment answers the human “let us” of pride (vv3–4). This divine council-language (echoing Genesis 1:26) acts to scatter what humanity united wrongly. The reversal comes at Pentecost: “All of them were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues as the Spirit enabled them” (Acts 2:4). Where the divine “let us” at Babel confused languages and scattered, the Spirit of the risen Christ descends (Acts 2:1–4) and unites the scattered nations in proclamation of “the wonders of God” (Acts 2:11).
8: The LORD scattered the builders across the whole earth and they stopped building. The city-building project of self-sufficient humanity is interrupted by divine judgment. The city God is building is different: “he was looking forward to the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God” (Hebrews 11:10 — of Abraham). The scattered peoples of Babel will be gathered again — not by human construction but by Christ’s cross: “I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself” (John 12:32). The divine scattering at Babel is reversed by the divine gathering at Calvary.
9: “Babel — because there the LORD confused the language of the whole world.” Babel (‘confusion’) is the name of the city that becomes in Scripture the symbol of all human civilization in opposition to God (Revelation 14:8; 17–18 — “Babylon the Great”). The “fall of Babylon” in Rev 18 is the fall of the Babel-principle — all human self-assertion against God. Christ’s victory over Babylon/Babel is announced by the angel: “Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!” (Revelation 18:2). The confusion that Babel names will be ended when Christ reigns and every tongue confesses him as Lord (Philippians 2:11) — a different kind of linguistic unity than Babel imagined.
10: The toledot of Shem begins the genealogy that leads from the flood to Abram — the genealogy that Luke 3:34–36 traces as the ancestry of Jesus. Shem, one hundred years old two years after the flood, fathers Arphaxad. Each link in this chain is a link in the genealogy of the Messiah. The post-flood genealogy is the narrowing of the line of promise — from all humanity (Genesis 10) to Shem’s line (Genesis 11) to Abram (12) to Isaac and Jacob, until the line narrows to one: Jesus of Nazareth, son of Abraham, son of David (Matthew 1:1).
11: Shem lives 500 years after Arphaxad’s birth. The post-flood lifespans begin to decline from the antediluvian figures (Methuselah: 969; Noah: 950) toward shorter spans. The shortening of lifespans across the genealogy reflects the post-flood world’s changed conditions. The death refrain from Genesis 5 continues — the mortality that Christ comes to defeat (1 Corinthians 15:26) persists through every generation of the Messianic genealogy, making the resurrection more urgent with each passing generation. Every death in this genealogy adds to the argument for the resurrection.
12: Arphaxad fathers Shelah at 35 years. Luke 3:35 names Arphaxad in Christ’s genealogy between Shem and Shelah (Luke uses the LXX’s “Cainan” as an intermediary, following a textual tradition). The genealogy moves at this point from the broad post-flood table toward the narrow Semitic line that leads to Abraham. Each generation is a generation closer to the incarnation — closer to the moment when the Son of God enters the genealogy as its culmination rather than its continuation.
13: Arphaxad lives 403 years after Shelah’s birth. The narrowing genealogy continues its slow movement toward Abraham. The centuries between the flood and Abraham’s call are centuries of patient divine purpose — God working through the ordinary mechanics of human generation to bring his Son into the world at exactly the right time (Galatians 4:4 — “when the fullness of time had come”). The divine patience across these 403 years is the same patience that 2 Peter 3:9 describes as the reason for the delay in Christ’s return.
14: Shelah fathers Eber at 30 years. Luke 3:35 names Shelah in Christ’s genealogy. Eber is the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews (ʿibrîm) — the name of the people through whom Christ comes. The very name of the Hebrew people, the people of God’s special covenant revelation, traces to this link in the genealogy. Christ is “born under the law” (Galatians 4:4) — born into the Hebrew people whose name begins with Eber.
15: Shelah lives 403 years after Eber’s birth. The genealogy continues its slow narrowing. The great spans of years in this chain — Shem to Abram spans roughly 390 years — represent the long centuries of divine patience and preparation between Noah and the Abrahamic covenant. The God who preserves the genealogical line through these centuries is the God who, “at just the right time… demonstrated his righteousness” through Christ (Romans 3:25–26). Every year of the genealogy is a year of divine faithfulness.
16: Eber fathers Peleg at 34 years. Luke 3:35 names Eber in Christ’s genealogy. Peleg’s generation is when “the earth was divided” (Genesis 10:25) — likely a reference to Babel’s linguistic scattering. Christ, who descends from the generation of the earth’s division, comes to gather what was divided: “he came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near” (Ephesians 2:17). The genealogy runs through Peleg’s divided generation toward the one who unites.
17: Eber lives 430 years after Peleg’s birth. The 430 years of Eber’s post-Peleg life is the same figure as Israel’s time in Egypt (Exodus 12:40–41). The coincidence of numbers may be unintentional but points to the way Scripture’s numbers often carry typological resonance. The 430 years of Egypt that end with the Exodus — the paradigmatic redemption of the OT — is the same span that Eber’s remaining life covers. The Exodus’s 430 years is the template that Christ’s greater exodus (Luke 9:31 — his “departure”) fulfills.
18: Peleg fathers Reu at 30 years. Luke 3:35 names Peleg in Christ’s genealogy. The genealogy moves from the divided-earth generation toward Abraham. Reu’s name may mean “friend” — echoing the relationship vocabulary that reaches its fullest expression in Christ: “I no longer call you servants… Instead I have called you friends” (John 15:15). The relational language embedded in these ancient names finds its ultimate expression in Christ’s redefining of the divine-human relationship as friendship.
19: Peleg lives 209 years after Reu’s birth. The lifespans continue to shorten — Peleg’s total of 239 years is less than half of the antediluvian figures. The shortening of life is the intensification of the mortality that the death refrain announced in Genesis 5. As lifespans shorten, the urgency of Christ’s victory over death increases. “Just as people are destined to die once, and after that to face judgment, so Christ was sacrificed once to take away the sins of many” (Hebrews 9:27–28). The trajectory of shortening life points toward the one who enters fully mortal life and defeats death from within.
20: Reu fathers Serug at 32 years. Luke 3:35 names Reu in Christ’s genealogy. The genealogy is now approximately halfway from Shem to Abram — the narrowing line moving steadily toward the man through whom God will announce the Messianic covenant. Every name in this genealogy is a name in the family tree of God incarnate. The Son of God will call these ancient names his ancestors — “born of the seed of David according to the flesh” (Romans 1:3) includes this entire pre-Davidic genealogy.
21: Reu lives 207 years after Serug’s birth. The shortening lifespan continues — Reu’s total of 239 years matches Peleg’s. Each generation brings the line closer to the one who will live only 33 years on earth but accomplish what centuries of long-lived patriarchs could not. The compression of human lifespan across these generations is the compression of the world’s history toward “the fullness of time” (Galatians 4:4) when God sends his Son born of a woman.
22: Serug fathers Nahor at 30 years. Luke 3:35 names Serug in Christ’s genealogy. The chain is nearly complete: Shem → Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → Reu → Serug → Nahor → Terah → Abraham. Three more links remain in the chain from Shem to Abram — and Abram to Christ is the story of the OT. The genealogy that begins with Shem ends with the man through whom God announces the gospel in advance (Galatians 3:8).
23: Serug lives 200 years after Nahor’s birth. Serug’s total lifespan is 230 years — continuing the decline from antediluvian figures. The shortening lives mark the acceleration of history toward its goal: not the end of all things but the coming of the one thing all things point to — Christ. “In these last days [God] has spoken to us by his Son” (Hebrews 1:2) — the “last days” begin with Christ’s coming; the genealogy is moving toward those last days with every generation.
24: Nahor fathers Terah at 29 years. Luke 3:34 names Nahor in Christ’s genealogy. Two more links remain: Terah fathers Abram, and then the Abrahamic covenant is announced. The approach of Abraham in the genealogy is the approach of the covenant that Paul calls “the gospel announced in advance” (Galatians 3:8). The reader of Luke’s genealogy knows that every step from Nahor toward Terah toward Abram is a step toward Jesus. The genealogy of Luke 3 is the genealogy of the one who walked through the same world these ancestors knew.
25: Nahor lives 119 years after Terah’s birth. Nahor’s total of 148 years is markedly shorter than any of his ancestors — the life of a man approaching the normal human lifespan of later generations. The world is changing; the genealogy is approaching its historical setting in the second millennium BC. God’s redemptive purpose moves through the changing conditions of human history — from the antediluvian world of Noah to the ancient Near East of Abraham — always faithful to the promise of the seed (Genesis 3:15).
26: Terah fathers Abram, Nahor, and Haran. The naming of Abram — the first of Terah’s sons, and the one through whom the covenant will run — is the penultimate step in the genealogy from Shem to the Messianic line. Luke 3:34 names Terah in Christ’s genealogy. The line has narrowed from all humanity (Genesis 10) to Shem’s descendants (Genesis 11) to Terah’s family (v26) to Abram alone (Genesis 12) to Isaac (Genesis 21) to Jacob (Genesis 25) to Judah (Genesis 38) — the progressive narrowing that culminates in the Davidic line and ultimately in Christ.
27: The toledot of Terah: Abram, Nahor, Haran; and Haran’s son Lot. The family of Terah is the family into which the Abrahamic covenant is about to be announced. Lot — Abram’s nephew — will travel with him and become a foil in the narrative: the man who chooses by sight (Sodom’s well-watered plain) rather than faith. The contrast between Abram (who walks by faith, Hebrews 11:8) and Lot (who walks by sight, 2 Corinthians 5:7’s negative image) is established in the family structure of this verse.
28: Haran dies in Ur while his father Terah is still alive — the tragedy of a child dying before a parent. Death does not wait for the proper order of generations; it strikes young and old, in the pre-Abraham world as in every world. The grief of Haran’s early death is the grief that Christ enters in John 11 when he stands before the tomb of Lazarus and weeps (John 11:35). Christ is “a man of sorrows, acquainted with grief” (Isaiah 53:3) — acquainted with exactly this kind of grief, the premature death of the beloved that disrupts the expected order of life.
29: Abram marries Sarai; Nahor marries Milcah. The marriages that establish the patriarchal family are the marriages through which the promise will flow. Sarai will be the mother of Isaac, through whom the covenant continues to Jacob and Judah and David and Christ. The marriage of Abram and Sarai is the marriage through which the Messianic line will run — the marriage Paul uses in Galatians 4:21–31 as an allegory of the two covenants: Hagar/Sinai vs. Sarah/the free Jerusalem, the latter being the covenant of promise through Christ.
30: “Now Sarai was childless because she was not able to conceive.” The barrenness of Sarai is the impossible condition that God will overcome to produce the promised heir. The pattern of barren women whose children God provides — Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth — reaches its ultimate form in Mary’s virginal conception of Christ. Each barren womb that God opens is a small step toward the impossible birth that defeats death itself. Paul in Galatians 4:27 quotes Isaiah 54:1 (“Rejoice, you who are barren”) and applies it to the Jerusalem above — the church born of grace, not human generation — exactly as Isaac was born of Sarai’s impossible conception.
31: Terah sets out from Ur of the Chaldeans toward Canaan — but stops at Haran. The journey toward the promised land begins with Terah but is only completed when Abram obeys God’s call (Genesis 12:4). Hebrews 11:8 says Abram obeyed “even though he did not know where he was going” — the model of faith-obedience that Christ embodies perfectly: “not as I will, but as you will” (Matthew 26:39). The incomplete journey of Terah (he stops at Haran and dies there) is completed by the son who trusts God’s word rather than settling for the comfortable interim stop.
32: Terah lives 205 years and dies in Haran — short of the promised land. The death of Terah in Haran — having set out for Canaan but not arrived — is the pattern of OT faith: “these were all commended for their faith, yet none of them received what had been promised” (Hebrews 11:39). Terah dies in sight of the promise. The faith that Abram will embody — leaving Haran after his father’s death (Acts 7:4) — is the faith that presses on past the place where others stop. Christ is the one who arrives at the destination that Terah died short of: “the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God” (Hebrews 11:10).
